beats.narration_text: Salaam. A learner asks: build me an interactive simulation of born approximation validity map. One Claude prompt. Here is what comes back.

props.command: build me an interactive sim of born approximation validity map

beats.narration_text: Born Approximation Validity Map is one of those ideas that is easier to see than to state. the core behaviour of born approximation validity map. This simulation makes that visible — and every educator can build one.
beats.new_visual_element: Concept card: Born Approximation Validity Map

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Concept card: Born Approximation Validity Map

beats.narration_text: Here is the prompt. One message to Claude.
beats.code_snippet: Build a single-file interactive HTML simulation of Born Approximation Validity Map. Use D3.js from CDN. All physics computed in-browser JavaScript. Sliders for: ka =  3.00  (dimensionless wavenumber), |V₀| = ξ =  1.00  (dimensionless strength). Verify: the key behaviour of born approximation validity map. Palette: background #FFFFFF, ink #2A1A0E, accent #C8102E.

props.command: Build a single-file interactive HTML simulation of Born Approximation Validity Map. Use D3.js from CDN. All physics computed in-browser JavaScript. Sliders for: ka =  3.00  (dimensionless wavenumber),

beats.narration_text: Claude returns a single-file HTML. Here is the heart of it — the integrator and the slider handler. No server. No build step. One file, everything in the browser.

beats.narration_text: At the default settings: born approximation validity map. Watch what the simulation shows.
Now drag ka =  3.00  (dimensionless wavenumber) from 0 to 1. Watch the physics respond in real time.
beats.new_visual_element: Slate: narrate slider sweep of ka =  3.00  (dimensionless wavenumber) from 0 to 1

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Slate: narrate slider sweep of ka =  3.00  (dimensionless wavenumber) from 0 to 1

beats.narration_text: the prediction holds — born approximation validity map behaves as the equations say. Exactly as the equations predict.
One prompt. A live demonstration of born approximation validity map. For educators: every concept in your course can have one of these. Claude builds it; you judge it; your students explore it.
Open this file in your browser and drag the controls. Or ask Claude to add a second parameter, change the palette, or explain the physics step by step alongside the simulation.
Take this prompt, run it on your own topic. Your turn.
beats.code_snippet: Take this Born Approximation Validity Map simulation and add a second slider that controls ka =  3.00  (dimensionless wavenumber). Show how changing it reveals a new aspect of the physics. Keep the single-file format and D3 CDN.
